ac:schema-version="1"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.query.es.node.port = <es.port>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li><li>Restart Teamwork Cloud services.</li></ol><p style="text-align: left;"><br />Now you can <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating indexing configurations" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[index resources for their element usages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in the Settings application and use the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Global element usage search" ri:space-key="MDTWRT" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[global element usage search functionality]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in a modeling tool.</p><h4 style="text-align: left;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Enabling Encrypted Communication (SSL) with ElasticSearch Server</span></h4><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="272e5fe1-6f54-4172-9a2e-ba86d49c5dee" ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:rich-text-body><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">SSL setup with Elasticsearch is a general security feature, required both to configure Elasticsearch itself and Teamwork Cloud. You must specify a username and password. See more information on setting up passwords in Elasticsearch: </span><a href="https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/7.16/security-minimal-setup.html" style="text-align: left;">https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/7.16/security-minimal-setup.html</a></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To enable encrypted communication between Teamwork Cloud and ElasticSearch servers</span></p><hr /><ol><li>Prepare type PCKS #12 (*.p12) certificate. You can:</li></ol><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Use an existing certificate for the machine that will host Elasticsearch, or</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Generate a new certificate for Elasticsearch using elasticsearch-certutil </span>(<a href="https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/certutil.html">https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/certutil.html</a>).</li></ul></li></ul><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Example:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="383d5e01-1327-47d9-9b6f-a4d0361393a9" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[elasticsearch-certutil cert --self-signed --name <elasticsearch.host.name>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;">Alternatively, if the certificate subject name does not match the hostname, you can generate the certificate to facilitate the <strong>Subject Alternative Name </strong>extension:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="08d3ed99-e33e-40ae-92ce-7eda5a2655b6" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[lasticsearch-certutil cert --self-signed --name <arbitrary.subject.name> --dns <elasticsearch.host.name> --ip <elasticsearch.host.ip>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>       2. Configure <em>elasticsearch.yml.</em></p><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;">Enable security:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="2c1350f6-ff3c-4f0f-bf87-f4528932950e" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[xpack.security.enabled: true]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;">Enable SSL for transport:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="80941c84-1392-4aa1-ab2b-3d8a7f51523f" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true
````html
xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode: certificate
xpack.security.transport.ssl.keystore.path: <path.to.p12.file>
xpack.security.transport.ssl.keystore.password: changeit]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;">Enable SSL for HTTP (REST API):</p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="056348e0-41b5-4600-a3d2-7f00cecba3dc" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true
xpack.security.http.ssl.verification_mode: certificate
xpack.security.http.ssl.keystore.path: <path.to.p12.file>
xpack.security.http.ssl.keystore.password: changeit]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="text-align: left;">       3. Import <span>the certificate into Java's trusted certificates keystore </span><em>cacerts</em><span>.</span></p><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;text-align: left;">First, export the X.509 certificate (usually *.crt or *.cer file) from the *.p12 file (for this use tools like <em>OpenSSL </em><span>or </span><em>KeyStore Explorer</em><span>). Then import the certificate file using Java's keytool:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="ca2a4510-4731-465c-98d2-64b0a04b4969" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[keytool.exe -import -file <path.to.certificate.file> -cacerts -alias <certificate.subject>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">       4. Set up Teamwork Cloud: i</span>n application.conc, set the following properties:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="5039d3e9-0172-4f6b-9606-69628e27aa53" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.query.es.security.enabled = true
esi.query.es.security.auth.username = "elastic"
esi.query.es.security.auth.password = "<elastic.password>"
esi.query.es.node.protocol = "https"]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h4><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Pointing multiple Teamwork Cloud instances to one Elasticsearch instance</span></h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Instead of using a separate <span>Elasticsearch instance for each Teamwork Cloud instance, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="82684633-a075-4058-b81b-47f51c8b016b">you can </ac:inline-comment-marker></span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="82684633-a075-4058-b81b-47f51c8b016b">point <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span>multiple </span>Teamwork Cloud instances or clusters to </span>a single Elasticsearch instance</ac:inline-comment-marker></span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="82684633-a075-4058-b81b-47f51c8b016b">.</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="bfdba542-ccf7-440e-9751-8e263d909b7d">To point multiple Teamwork Cloud instances to one Elasticsearch instance</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Go to <em><span>&lt;install_root&gt;/TeamworkCloud/configuration</span></em>, open the <strong>application.conf</strong> file.</span></li><li data-uuid="ac96f46f-42c0-4304-b3ce-8615323a17af"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c0bc5bff-8662-4538-b06c-834c5702f9a8">Uncomment the following setting and specify a valid suffix. Note that <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">each Teamwork Cloud instance pointing to the same Elasticsearch instance needs a unique suffix:</span></ac:inline-comment-marker></span></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="d1c6cd75-3927-483b-87d9-0819ed283df7" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# Custom suffix for Elasticsearch index names. Allows for separating indexes of separate TWCloud instances/clusters
# if they are using same Elasticsearch instance. Uncomment and enter a non-empty alphanumeric value to enable.
# NOTE: make sure to set the same suffix for all nodes of the same TWCloud cluster!
#
# esi.query.es.index-name-suffix = "CHANGE_ME"]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247046630 space=MCS version=8 -->
## PAGE 00715: Hardening Cassandra

- page_id: `247046630`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247046630/Hardening+Cassandra
- version_number: 8
- version_date: `2026-01-08T08:54:11.974+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Configuration > Hardening the deployment and its services
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Used scripts**

Download the *harden_cassandra_ports.sh* script [here](https://docs.nomagic.com/MCS/latest/magic-collaboration-studio/downloadable-scripts-283246728.html).

##### Cassandra Authentication

By default, Cassandra is deployed with the AllowAllAuthenticator. This authenticator, as the name implies, is an anonymous authenticator which performs no checks.

If you require authenticated connections, you will need to make changes to your **cassandra.yaml** and change the authenticator to PasswordAuthenticator. If you are running a multi-node Cassandra cluster, you need to change the replication factor of the system_auth keyspace.

Detailed instructions can be found at [https://docs.datastax.com/en/ddacsecurity/doc/ddacsecurity/secureConfigNativeAuth.html](https://docs.datastax.com/en/ddacsecurity/doc/ddacsecurity/secureConfigNativeAuth.html)

After making the changes to Cassandra, you will need to update the Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudconfigurations to utilize authentication.

The required changes are as follows:

```text

```

Where *newcassandrauser* and *newcassandrapassword* correspond to the user and credentials which you configured in Cassandra.

###### SSL authentication for Cassandra

If the Cassandra certificate is**not signed by a public certificate authority**, import the Cassandra public certificateto the product's Java cacertsby running the following command:

```bash

```

**Java cacerts default location****for the product**(example):*/etc/alternatives/jre_17/lib/security/cacerts*

##### Cassandra Encrypted Connections

Starting from Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudversion 2021x, encrypted communication with Cassandra is supported. To achieve this, the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deployment configuration' space=''] is needed.

##### Data Encryption at Rest

Open source Cassandra does not support encryption at rest. If you require encryption at rest, you need to use a disk encryption layer.

##### Configuration file property encryption

Apache Cassandra (open source version) does not support encrypting cassandra.yaml properties values.

##### Port access for the firewall

When installing on Linux using our deployment scripts, all of the ports required by Cassandra for inter-node communication, as well as for the Teamwork Cloud nodes to communicate with Cassandra nodes, are opened globally. This configuration is deployed mostly to facilitate testing of the environment upon installation, prior to taking any measures to harden the installation. If we check the firewall upon installation, we will see an output similar to the one below:

```text

```

In our deployment, we create a firewall service definition to facilitate the management of the rules. This file is located in */etc/firewalld/services/cassandra.xml*, and contains the following:

```text
cassandra
    cassandra
    
    
        
        
        


]]>
```

The first step in securing Cassandra is to limit traffic only to Cassandra and Teamwork Cloud nodes. In the example below, we have a single-node Cassandra/product installation, with its IP address set to 10.254.254.56.

As can be seen below, the firewall configuration has been modified to only allow access from itself.

```text

```

The process to follow is to remove the general firewall allowance to the Cassandra service. After that, we want to ensure that if direct port rule assignments were made, they are removed. Finally, we want to create a set of rich rules that will allow access only to the required nodes.

If your deployment consists of a single-node or multi-node cluster where both Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudand Cassandra reside on the same nodes, automate this process by [downloading](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCS/Downloadable+scripts)and using the *harden_cassandra_ports.sh* script. The script is structured in such a way that it will output the rich rule commands to the screen to facilitate copying and modifying to add nodes, which is needed if you have Teamwork Cloud nodes that are not part of the Cassandra cluster.

The *harden_cassandra_ports.sh* script should be executed on all nodes of a multi-node Cassandra cluster, with all nodes being in an operational state.

For Windows users: Create firewall rules restricting access on the aforementioned ports only to authorized nodes - Cassandra nodes and Teamwork Cloud nodes.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Used scripts</strong></p><p>Download the <em>harden_cassandra_ports.sh</em> script <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/MCS/latest/magic-collaboration-studio/downloadable-scripts-283246728.html">here</a>.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Cassandra Authentication</span></h3><p>By default, Cassandra is deployed with the AllowAllAuthenticator. This authenticator, as the name implies, is an anonymous authenticator which performs no checks.</p><p>If you require authenticated connections, you will need to make changes to your <strong>cassandra.yaml</strong> and change the authenticator to PasswordAuthenticator. If you are running a multi-node Cassandra cluster, you need to change the replication factor of the system_auth keyspace.</p><p>Detailed instructions can be found at <a href="https://docs.datastax.com/en/ddacsecurity/doc/ddacsecurity/secureConfigNativeAuth.html">https://docs.datastax.com/en/ddacsecurity/doc/ddacsecurity/secureConfigNativeAuth.html</a></p><p>After making the changes to Cassandra, you will need to update the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>configurations to utilize authentication.</p><p>The required changes are as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6bda044e-e33a-4315-a378-87de036a7130"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">application.conf</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[auth-provider {
     # Enable this section to enable the cassandra authentication.
     class = PlainTextAuthProvider
     username = newcassandrauser
     password = newcassandrapassword
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Where <em>newcassandrauser</em> and <em>newcassandrapassword</em> correspond to the user and credentials which you configured in Cassandra.</p><h4>SSL authentication for Cassandra</h4><p><span>If the Cassandra certificate is</span><span> </span><strong>not signed by a public certificate authority</strong><span>, import the Cassandra public certificate </span><span>to the product's Java cacerts </span><span>by running the following command:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9fea3b67-aa63-4e76-a75a-1878c4bb8957"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[keytool.exe -import -trustcacerts -keystore cacerts -storepass changeit -noprompt -alias 'server-alias' -file server.cer]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>Java cacerts default location </strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><strong>for the product </strong>(example</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">): </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">/etc/alternatives/jre_17/lib/security/cacerts</em></p><h3>Cassandra Encrypted Connections</h3><p>Starting from <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>version 2021x, encrypted communication with Cassandra is supported. To achiev<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="98bf404f-b92c-4795-b027-182f684c920f">e this,</ac:inline-comment-marker> the <ac:link ac:anchor="Configuring secure SSL communication between Teamwork Cloud and Cassandra"><ri:page ri:content-title="Deployment configuration" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[configuration of secure SSL communication]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> is needed.</p><h3><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="67214981-c66f-453e-840f-ecc541affddf">Data Encryption at Rest</ac:inline-comment-marker></h3><p>Open source Cassandra does not support encryption at rest. If you require encryption at rest, you need to use a disk encryption layer.</p><h3>Configuration file property encryption</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Apache Cassandra (open source version) does not support encrypting cassandra.yaml properties values.</span></p><h3>Port access for the firewall</h3><p>When installing on Linux using our deployment scripts, all of the ports required by Cassandra for inter-node communication, as well as for the Teamwork Cloud nodes to communicate with Cassandra nodes, are opened globally. This configuration is deployed mostly to facilitate testing of the environment upon installation, prior to taking any measures to harden the installation. If we check the firewall upon installation, we will see an output similar to the one below:</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bd77a12c-ab8a-4d19-913f-8c564b224951"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# firewall-cmd --list-all
public (active)
  target: default
  icmp-block-inversion: no
  interfaces: eth0
  sources:
  services: cassandra lmadmin ssh twcloud
  ports:
  protocols:
  masquerade: no
  forward-ports:
  source-ports:
  icmp-blocks:
  rich rules:


]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In our deployment, we create a firewall service definition to facilitate the management of the rules. This file is located in <em>/etc/firewalld/services/cassandra.xml</em>, and contains the following:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5d4e3771-a365-4d1b-9eff-877db6449eae"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# cat /etc/firewalld/services/cassandra.xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<service version="1.0">
    <short>cassandra</short>
    <description>cassandra</description>
    <port port="7000" protocol="tcp"/>
    <port port="7001" protocol="tcp"/>
        <port port="9042" protocol="tcp"/>
        <port port="9160" protocol="tcp"/>
        <port port="9142" protocol="tcp"/>
</service>

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The first step in securing Cassandra is to limit traffic only to Cassandra and Teamwork Cloud nodes. In the example below, we have a single-node Cassandra/product installation, with its IP address set to 10.254.254.56.</p><p>As can be seen below, the firewall configuration has been modified to only allow access from itself.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f056107c-d95f-4d93-870b-dc3a8af27d77"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# firewall-cmd --list-all
public (active)
  target: default
  icmp-block-inversion: no
  interfaces: eth0
  sources:
  services: lmadmin ssh twcloud
  ports:
  protocols:
  masquerade: no
  forward-ports:
  source-ports:
  icmp-blocks:
  rich rules:
        rule family="ipv4" source address="10.254.254.56" service name="cassandra" accept

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The process to follow is to remove the general firewall allowance to the Cassandra service. After that, we want to ensure that if direct port rule assignments were made, they are removed.  Finally, we want to create a set of rich rules that will allow access only to the required nodes.</p><p>If your deployment consists of a single-node or multi-node cluster where both <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>and Cassandra reside on the same nodes, automate this process by <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCS/Downloadable+scripts"> downloading </a>and using the <em>harden_cassandra_ports.sh</em> script. The script is structured in such a way that it will output the rich rule commands to the screen to facilitate copying and modifying to add nodes, which is needed if you have Teamwork Cloud nodes that are not part of the Cassandra cluster.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8b5c56b3-de21-478c-9c44-2dc920964c70"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <em>harden_cassandra_ports.sh</em> script should be executed on all nodes of a multi-node Cassandra cluster, with all nodes being in an operational state.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="327ecc3e-fa51-4949-b474-aef3d8c51f32"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For Windows users: Create firewall rules restricting access on the aforementioned ports only to authorized nodes - Cassandra nodes and Teamwork Cloud nodes.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247046632 space=MCS version=10 -->
## PAGE 00716: Hardening the deployment

- page_id: `247046632`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247046632/Hardening+the+deployment
- version_number: 10
- version_date: `2025-12-18T14:35:58.816+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Configuration > Hardening the deployment and its services
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Used files**

- [ATTACHMENT filename='twc.java.security']

##### Protocols and Ciphers

falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloudtwcconsists of Java-based services - Teamwork Cloud (twcloud) and WebApp (webapp).

Teamwork Cloud requires a supported Java version (see [System requirements](https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=system-requirements&version=latest)) (Java location varies depending on how it was deployed and configured), whereas Web Application Platform uses a bundled Java, located in*<install_root>/WebAppPlatform**/jre/*.

Therefore, in order to harden these services, we must begin by hardening the JVM. The default settings for the JVM are located in **java.security**.

#### EXPAND: How to Check Protocols and Ciphers for a Specific Port

How to Check Protocols and Ciphers for a Specific Port

We can check the ciphers/protocols being used by the applications using nmap (version 7.x) or TestSSLServer.jar, available from [https://community.rsa.com/s/article/How-to-check-for-available-SSL-TLS-protocols-and-ciphers-for-a-specific-port-in-RSA-Authentication-Manager-8-x](https://community.rsa.com/s/article/How-to-check-for-available-SSL-TLS-protocols-and-ciphers-for-a-specific-port-in-RSA-Authentication-Manager-8-x).

As an example, below is a scan using both tools against a default installation. In this example, we will be testing port 8111, the Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudport.

```text

```

As can be observed above, the default configuration using OpenJDK 1.8.0_242 allows TLS v1.0 and v1.1, which are deprecated. Additionally, we can see that several key exchanges are taking place using dh1024.

We then proceed to harden the configuration.

Since we are dealing with ciphers, you need to make sure that you do not disable a cipher required by your certificates.

After hardening the VM, we end up with a different set of allowed ciphers and protocols, as shown below.

```text

```

The process of hardening the JVM requires making some changes to the **java.security** file. While these can be made directly, the downside is that if you upgrade your JVM, you will have to reapply your changes.

However, we can place our modifications in our own file, and simply pass a parameter to the JVM upon invocation so that it will apply our changes.

For example, we can create a file *<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/jmx.password*, and pass a parameter to the JVM in the form of *-Djava.security.properties=*<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/twc.java.security*.*

Our hardened security settings are as shown below:

```text

```

To apply these settings, we need to make changes to both the Teamwork Cloud and Web App Platform services.

1. **Teamwork Cloud service** 
For Linux, edit*<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/jvm.options*and add a line as shown below: jvm.options/TeamworkCloud/configuration/twc.java.security]]> For Windows, edit the registry key *Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\TeamworkCloud\Parameters\Java\Options* and append the setting pointing to your security overrides to the bottom of the settings.
2. **Web App Platform service** 
For Linux,*<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/bin/setenv.sh*and add the directive to the**JVM_OPTS**variable as shown below: setenv.sh/TeamworkCloud/configuration/twc.java.security"]]> On Windows, you need to edit the registry key *Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\WebApp\Parameters\Java\Options* and append the setting pointing to your security overrides to the bottom of the settings.

JMX

By default, the Teamwork Cloud service activates a JMX remote port to facilitate application monitoring. The default configuration does not contain any form of authentication.

On Linux, the configuration is located in *<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/jvm.options*.

On Windows, it is located in the registry key *Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\TeamworkCloud\Parameters\Java\Options*.

```text

```

These settings can be removed, thereby removing JMX remote access.

If you would like to allow remote JMX access but require authentication, you can do so by adding settings. For complete documentation, please refer to the Java documentation.

As an example, the below configuration adds password authentication:

```text
/TeamworkCloud/configuration/jmx.password
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.access.file=/TeamworkCloud/configuration/jmx.access
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.ssl=false]]>
```

As can be seen, we are pointing to a set of files (*<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/jmx.password* and *<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/twc.java.security*) that control who can access these files.

The vulnerability vector is one whereby JMX could be exploited to execute code. To prevent this, we allow only an authenticated user (jmx.password) who has read-only rights (jmx.access).

```text

```

```text

```

In this example, we created a user (monitoring) with a password (DqzbksT4ET), who can only read values via Remote JMX, but cannot write or execute anything via JMX.

The password and access files have a very stringent ownership requirement. They need to be owned by the user running the process and be accessible exclusively to that user.

In the default installation, the Teamwork Cloud service must run under the Teamwork Cloud (twcloud) user account. All related files must be owned by the twcloud user and must have full permissions (rwx) granted exclusively to this user. No other users are permitted to have any access to these files.

# ll jmx.* 
-rwx------. 1 twcloud twcloud 20 Mar 16 15:11 jmx.access 
-rwx------. 1 twcloud twcloud 26 Apr 21 10:41 jmx.password

The default settings for the JVM are located in <strong>java.security</strong>. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="58d87dcd-e8d2-4242-a939-315d9bf88e79"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">How to Check Protocols and Ciphers for a Specific Port</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>We can check the ciphers/protocols being used by the applications using nmap (version 7.x) or TestSSLServer.jar, available from <a style="text-align: left;" href="https://community.rsa.com/s/article/How-to-check-for-available-SSL-TLS-protocols-and-ciphers-for-a-specific-port-in-RSA-Authentication-Manager-8-x" class="" rel="nofollow">https://community.rsa.com/s/article/How-to-check-for-available-SSL-TLS-protocols-and-ciphers-for-a-specific-port-in-RSA-Authentication-Manager-8-x</a>.</p><p>As an example, below is a scan using both tools against a default installation. In this example, we will be testing port 8111, the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>port.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="798c7a79-116d-4564-a5ed-dd4df3dbe396"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 8111 127.0.0.1

````html
  71ed3969e41a94877c51aacb87d995af4a12b6d9: CN=x.x.x.x
----------------------
Minimal encryption strength:     strong encryption (96-bit or more)
Achievable encryption strength:  strong encryption (96-bit or more)
BEAST status: protected
CRIME status: protected

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>The process of hardening the JVM requires making some changes to the</span> <strong> java.security</strong> <span> file. While these can be made directly, the downside is that if you upgrade your JVM, you will have to reapply your changes.</span></p><p>However, we can place our modifications in our own file, and simply pass a parameter to the JVM upon invocation so that it will apply our changes.</p><p>For example, we can create a file <em style="text-align: left;">&lt;install_root&gt;/TeamworkCloud/configuration/jmx.password</em>, and pass a parameter to the JVM in the form of <em>-Djava.security.properties=<em style="text-align: left;">&lt;install_root&gt;/TeamworkCloud/configuration/twc.java.security</em>.</em></p><p>Our hardened security settings are as shown below:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="256f442c-3904-48d3-a5d6-77d4180568b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">twc.java.security</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[jdk.tls.disabledAlgorithms=SSLv3, TLSv1, TLSv1.1, RC4, DES, MD5withRSA, DH keySize < 2048, \
    EC keySize < 224, 3DES_EDE_CBC, anon, RSA keySize < 2048, SHA1, DHE, NULL
jdk.tls.ephemeralDHKeySize=2048
jdk.tls.rejectClientInitiatedRenegotiation=true]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To apply these settings, we need to make changes to both the Teamwork Cloud and Web App Platform services.</p><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Teamwork Cloud service</strong><br /><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="347c4002-6646-4cba-90d8-a7007da29e3a">For Linux, edit </ac:inline-comment-marker><em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="9e3d728c-f410-4343-a466-31bd9e59f04c"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="347c4002-6646-4cba-90d8-a7007da29e3a">&lt;install_root&gt;/TeamworkCloud/jvm.options</ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker></em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="347c4002-6646-4cba-90d8-a7007da29e3a"> and add a line as shown below:</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="95a5eec9-e510-4b2e-83ba-58305a4e045d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">jvm.options</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[-Djava.security.properties=<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/twc.java.security]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">For Windows, edit the registry key <em>Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\TeamworkCloud\Parameters\Java\Options</em> and append the setting pointing to your security overrides to the bottom of the settings.</p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Web App Platform service</strong><br /><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="cfe16323-f71f-4b72-9229-6425383455a1">For Linux, </ac:inline-comment-marker><em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="cfe16323-f71f-4b72-9229-6425383455a1">&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/bin/setenv.sh</ac:inline-comment-marker></em><span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="cfe16323-f71f-4b72-9229-6425383455a1"> and add the directive to the </ac:inline-comment-marker></span><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="cfe16323-f71f-4b72-9229-6425383455a1">JVM_OPTS</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="cfe16323-f71f-4b72-9229-6425383455a1"> variable as shown below:</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fd75d0d2-dc1e-47c0-abe3-47315042c495"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">setenv.sh</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[JVM_OPTS="-server -XX:+UseParallelGC  -Xms4096M -Xmx8192M -Djava.security.properties=<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/twc.java.security"]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">On Windows, you need to edit the registry key <em>Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\WebApp\Parameters\Java\Options</em> and append the setting pointing to your security overrides to the bottom of the settings.</p></li></ol><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="font-size: 16.0px;font-weight: bold;letter-spacing: -0.006em;">JMX</span></p><p>By default, the Teamwork Cloud service activates a JMX remote port to facilitate application monitoring. The default configuration does not contain any form of authentication.</p><p>On Linux, the configuration is located in <em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">&lt;install_root&gt;/TeamworkCloud/jvm.options</span></em>.</p><p>On Windows, it is located in the registry key <em>Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\TeamworkCloud\Parameters\Java\Options</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ee0e6979-fec2-409c-a91f-f875354235f4"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.port=2468
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.rmi.port=2468
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.local.only=false
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.authenticate=false
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.ssl=false]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>These settings can be removed, thereby removing JMX remote access.</p><p>If you would like to allow remote JMX access but require authentication, you can do so by adding settings. For complete documentation, please refer to the Java documentation.</p><p>As an example, the below configuration adds password authentication:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bfa4d566-8587-4912-a860-07c8023eefdb"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.port=2468
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.rmi.port=2468
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.local.only=false
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.authenticate=true
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.password.file=<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/jmx.password
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.access.file=<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/jmx.access
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.ssl=false]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>As can be seen, we are pointing to a set of files (<em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">&lt;install_root&gt;/TeamworkCloud/configuration/jmx.password</span></em> and <em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">&lt;install_root&gt;/TeamworkCloud/configuration/twc.java.security</span></em>) that control who can access these files.</p><p>The vulnerability vector is one whereby JMX could be exploited to execute code. To prevent this, we allow only an authenticated user (jmx.password) who has read-only rights (jmx.access).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f86ad40c-fd02-431b-a566-612bef7211f5"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">jmx.password</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[monitoring  DqzbksT4ET]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="71cedc20-ff24-4541-84f1-cba195e11ec8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">jmx.access</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[monitoring readonly]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In this example, we created a user (monitoring) with a password (DqzbksT4ET), who can only read values via Remote JMX, but cannot write or execute anything via JMX.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="38d23f67-a185-4e56-8663-0774e38f386c"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The password and access files have a very stringent ownership requirement. They need to be owned by the user running the process and be accessible exclusively to that user.</p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">In the default installation, the Teamwork Cloud service must run under the Teamwork Cloud (twcloud) user account. All related files must be owned by the twcloud user and must have full permissions (rwx) granted exclusively to this user. No other users are permitted to have any access to these files.</span></p><p># ll jmx.*<br />-rwx------. 1 twcloud twcloud 20 Mar 16 15:11 jmx.access<br />-rwx------. 1 twcloud twcloud 26 Apr 21 10:41 jmx.password</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247046625 space=MCS version=6 -->
## PAGE 00717: Hardening the deployment and its services

- page_id: `247046625`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247046625/Hardening+the+deployment+and+its+services
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2025-12-03T14:54:32.941+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Configuration
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The default shipping configuration of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudis not a hardened configuration. When hardening an installation, some variables can render the installation inoperative, such as incompatibility of the supported ciphers in a certificate and the supported ciphers in the hardened configuration. Furthermore, the default configurations assume that the deployment is behind a secure infrastructure, and therefore, required ports are globally allowed.

Since some of the product's infrastructure relies on available components, newly discovered vulnerabilities need to be mitigated during the life-cycle of the installation.

The sections below cover potentially exploitable vulnerabilities of the different components, as well as various steps to mitigate them depending on the policies of the deploying organization.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The default shipping configuration of <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>is not a hardened configuration. When hardening an installation, some variables can render the installation inoperative, such as incompatibility of the supported ciphers in a certificate and the supported ciphers in the hardened configuration. Furthermore, the default configurations assume that the deployment is behind a secure infrastructure, and therefore, required ports are globally allowed.</p><p>Since some of the product's infrastructure relies on available components, newly discovered vulnerabilities need to be mitigated during the life-cycle of the installation.</p><p>The sections below cover potentially exploitable vulnerabilities of the different components, as well as various steps to mitigate them depending on the policies of the deploying organization.</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="1af390c3-7a92-4d14-bb6a-49fc02d6d986" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247046634 space=MCS version=9 -->
## PAGE 00718: Hardening Web Application Platform

- page_id: `247046634`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247046634/Hardening+Web+Application+Platform
- version_number: 9
- version_date: `2026-03-09T12:42:02.302+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Configuration > Hardening the deployment and its services
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Used scripts**

Download the *upgrade_tomcat_webapp.sh* and*upgrade_jdk_webapp.sh* scripts [here](https://docs.nomagic.com/MCS/latest/magic-collaboration-studio/downloadable-scripts-283246728.html).

The Web Application Platform, as deployed by our installer, runs on a bundled Apache Tomcat. As such, best practices for hardening Apache Tomcat should be followed.

Although we have already constrained ciphers and protocols at the JVM level, it is best practice to do so at the Tomcat configuration level. We also need to address issues such as secure cookies, disable XSS on foreign sites, and also remove default directories published as part of the default installation. The official Tomcat documentation covers a large portion of this (recommended reading) - [Tomcat Security Considerations](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-10.1-doc/security-howto.html). Additionally, there are a plethora of documents online covering all aspects of securing Tomcat to OWASP standards.

##### server.xml

Follow steps below to make changes to *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/conf/server.xml.*

1. Disable the AJP connector unless you specifically intend to use it by doing the following: 
Replace the following lines: ]]> with the lines below: <Connector port="8009" protocol="AJP/1.3" redirectPort="8443" />]]>
2. Prevent the Tomcat instance from advertising what server is being used when an error is encountered by doing the hollowing:Add the following lines at the end of the file, right above the closing </Host> tag. ]]>

- The shutdown port 8005 is disabled by default. If you need to enable it, we recommend to change the default SHUTDOWN password. To enable the port 8005, open the <install_root>/WebAppPlatform/conf/server.xml file and do the following: Replace the following line: ]]> with the line below: ]]>

- The port 8080 with redirection is disabled by default. To enable it open the <install_root>/WebAppPlatform/conf/server.xml file and uncomment the following lines: -->]]>

##### web.xml

Having completed the configuration of server.xml, we will now proceed to configure *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/conf/web.xml.*

We need to ensure that cookies are constrained to HTTPS.

```text
30
		
		true
		true
		
    ]]>
```

You only need to insert the following lines:

**<cookie-config>**

**<http-only>true</http-only>**

**<secure>true</secure>**

**</cookie-config>**

##### Tomcat installation

Having made the necessary changes to the configuration files, we will now proceed to remove all of the default applications in the tomcat distribution, which could expose our installation to external vulnerabilities.

If we look at a directory of *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/webapps*, we will see the following:

```text

```

As you can see, in addition to *webapp.war* and the *webapp* directory, there are additional directories, containing applications, which could potentially be exploited.

You want to remove *docs*, *examples*, *host-manager*, *manager*, and *ROOT*.

When you remove the ROOT application directory, accessing [https://ip_address:8443](https://ip_address:8443) will no longer display the Apache Tomcat default landing page.

##### Upgrading and patching Apache Tomcat

Web Application Platform uses Apache Tomcat as the Java web application server for deployment. Upgrading or patching is often required to keep your Tomcat server up to date with all the latest security and performance improvements.

Refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''] page for version compatibility between Web Application Platform and Apache Tomcat releases.

The files required for upgrading and patching Tomcat are a compilation of JAR files residing in*<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/bin* and *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/lib*.

Always make a backup of these directories before performing any upgrade or patch.

For Linux server deployment, use the*upgrade_tomcat_webapp.sh*script to automatically update your instance to the target Tomcat version (set in script). This script can perform installation either from a locally-stored package or pull from Apache’s server (internet access required).

To upgrade Apache Tomcat on Linux using script

1. Download *upgrade_tomcat_webapp.sh* script.
2. For a server with internet access:
  1. Execute the script and specify the desired Tomcat version when prompted.

**For offline installation:**

1. 
  1. Download the Apache Tomcat package from https://archive.apache.org/dist/tomcat (tar.gz and zip files are both supported.)
  2. Execute the script and confirm installation from a local package.

To manually "slip-stream" an update without having to fully replace the Tomcat installation, replace the existing JAR files in these directories with ones from the newer Apache Tomcat package. After replacing the JAR files, make sure the new files’ permission and ownership are same as before.

##### Upgrading Web Application Platform bundled JRE

To automatically upgrade the JRE, download and run the *upgrade_jdk_webapp.sh* script. When prompted, provide the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Used scripts</strong></p><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Download the <em>upgrade_tomcat_webapp.sh</em> and </span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><em>upgrade_jdk_webapp.sh</em> scripts <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/MCS/latest/magic-collaboration-studio/downloadable-scripts-283246728.html">here</a>.</span></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>The Web Application Platform, as deployed by our installer, runs on a bundled Apache Tomcat. As such, best practices for hardening Apache Tomcat should be followed.</p><p>Although we have already constrained ciphers and protocols at the JVM level, it is best practice to do so at the Tomcat configuration level. We also need to address issues such as secure cookies, disable XSS on foreign sites, and also remove default directories published as part of the default installation. The official Tomcat documentation covers a large portion of this (recommended reading) - <a href="https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-10.1-doc/security-howto.html">Tomcat Security Considerations</a>. Additionally, there are a plethora of documents online covering all aspects of securing Tomcat to OWASP standards.</p><h3><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="cbe49ad2-9cab-4240-996c-2b41010d4e3a">server.xml</ac:inline-comment-marker></h3><p>Follow steps below to make changes to <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/conf/server.xml.</em></p><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Disable the AJP connector unless you specifically intend to use it by doing the following:<br />Replace the following lines:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="021ea196-3506-409f-89c0-4cfe02646c69"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<!-- Define an AJP 1.3 Connector on port 8009 -->
    <Connector port="8009" protocol="AJP/1.3" redirectPort="8443" />]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">with the lines below:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="214a86c4-2f38-4d05-8870-a6116a9596d7"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[   <!-- Define an AJP 1.3 Connector on port 8009 -->
   <!--
   <Connector port="8009" protocol="AJP/1.3" redirectPort="8443" />
   -->]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><span>Prevent the Tomcat instance from advertising what server is being used when an error is encountered by doing the hollowing:<br /></span>Add the following lines at the end of the file, right above the closing &lt;/Host&gt; tag.<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="36923ab5-33ba-4e13-9b8b-b16fcd32f9a7"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[        <!-- Suppress server name on internal error pages -->
        <Valve className="org.apache.catalina.valves.ErrorReportValve" showReport="false" showServerInfo="false" />
      </Host>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><p><br /><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2ae9c1f1-6330-4af5-bc68-685cc9421be3"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>The shutdown port 8005 is disabled by default. If you need to enable it, we recommend <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">to change the default SHUTDOWN password. </span>To enable the port 8005, open the &lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/conf/server.xml file and do the following:<br />Replace the following line:<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="43fcbf1c-05b1-4b51-a189-8a1fce935306"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<Server port="-1" shutdown="SHUTDOWN">]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro>with the line below:<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="649453b2-7d07-4cef-a7de-c0177c0bb34c"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<Server port="8005" shutdown="SHUTDOWN">]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul><ul><li>The port 8080 with redirection is disabled by default. To enable it open the &lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/conf/server.xml file and uncomment the following lines:<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="38849bd9-4f23-4623-86a6-d94c37bc707a"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<!--
    <Connector executor="tomcatThreadPool"
               port="8080" protocol="HTTP/1.1"
               connectionTimeout="20000"
               Server=" "
               redirectPort="8443" />
-->]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><span>web.xml</span></h3><p>Having completed the configuration of server.xml, we will now proceed to configure <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/conf/web.xml.</em></p><p><span>We need to ensure that cookies are constrained to HTTPS.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="40976019-4bf7-43a6-81e2-5d625d5160ef"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[  <!-- ==================== Default Session Configuration ================= -->
  <!-- You can set the default session timeout (in minutes) for all newly   -->
  <!-- created sessions by modifying the value below.                       -->

    <session-config>
        <session-timeout>30</session-timeout>
		<cookie-config>
		<http-only>true</http-only>
		<secure>true</secure>
		</cookie-config>
    </session-config>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aa1cc0fb-fae0-4822-aaa9-385320085835"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You only need to insert the following lines:</p><p><strong>&lt;cookie-config&gt; </strong></p><p><strong>&lt;http-only&gt;true&lt;/http-only&gt; </strong></p><p><strong>&lt;secure&gt;true&lt;/secure&gt; </strong></p><p><strong>&lt;/cookie-config&gt;</strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><br />Tomcat installation</h3><p>Having made the necessary changes to the configuration files, we will now proceed to remove all of the default applications in the tomcat distribution, which could expose our installation to external vulnerabilities.</p><p>If we look at a directory of <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/webapps</em>, we will see the following:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6d44d74b-3d19-4eea-a89e-9cf6cf1a06ed"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[drwxrwxr-x. 14 twcloud twcloud     4096 Apr 15 14:39 docs
drwxrwxr-x.  6 twcloud twcloud       83 Apr 15 14:39 examples
drwxrwxr-x.  5 twcloud twcloud       87 Apr 15 14:39 host-manager
drwxrwxr-x.  5 twcloud twcloud      103 Apr 15 14:39 manager
drwxrwxr-x.  3 twcloud twcloud      283 Apr 15 14:39 ROOT
drwxr-x---.  8 twcloud twcloud      117 Apr 15 14:47 webapp
-rwxrwxr-x.  1 twcloud twcloud 67742880 Oct 31 17:56 webapp.war]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">As you can see, in addition to <em>webapp.war</em> and the <em>webapp</em> directory, there are additional directories, containing applications, which could potentially be exploited.</p><p>You want to remove <em>docs</em>, <em>examples</em>, <em>host-manager</em>, <em>manager</em>, and <em>ROOT</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1ad187e5-4954-4cf5-bfd7-0ef333aaf936"><ac:rich-text-body><p>When you remove the ROOT application directory, accessing <a href="https://ip_address:8443">https://ip_address:8443</a> will no longer display the Apache Tomcat default landing page.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 style="text-align: left;">Upgrading and patching Apache Tomcat</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Web Application Platform uses Apache Tomcat as the Java web application server for deployment. Upgrading or patching is often required to keep your Tomcat server up to date with all the latest security and performance improvements.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Refer to <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Third-Party Component Compatibility" /></ac:link> page for version compatibility between Web Application Platform and Apache Tomcat releases.</span></p><p style="text-align: left;">The files required for upgrading and patching Tomcat are a compilation of JAR files residing in<span> </span><em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/bin</em> and <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/lib</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="154f24fe-9343-4663-94fd-cec731505a67"><ac:rich-text-body><p style="text-align: left;">Always make a backup of these directories before performing any upgrade or patch.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">For Linux server deployment, use the<span> </span></span><em>upgrade_tomcat_webapp.sh</em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span> </span>script to automatically update your instance to the target Tomcat version (set in script). This script can perform installation either from a locally-stored package or pull from Apache’s server (internet access required).</span></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">To upgrade Apache Tomcat on Linux using script</span></p><hr /><ol><li class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Download <em>upgrade_tomcat_webapp.sh</em> script.</span></li><li class="auto-cursor-target"><strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">For a server with internet access:</span></strong><ol><li class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the script and specify the desired Tomcat version when prompted.</li></ol></li></ol><p>          <strong>For offline installation:</strong></p><ol><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ol><li>Download the Apache Tomcat package from<span> </span><a href="https://archive.apache.org/dist/tomcat" title="Follow link">https://archive.apache.org/dist/tomcat</a><span> </span>(tar.gz and zip files are both supported.)</li><li>Execute the script and confirm installation from a local package.</li></ol></li></ol><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">To manually &quot;slip-stream&quot; an update without having to fully replace the Tomcat installation, replace the existing JAR files in these directories with ones from the newer Apache Tomcat package. After replacing the JAR files, make sure the new files’ permission and ownership are same as before.</span></p><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Upgrading Web Application Platform bundled JRE</h3><p>To automatically upgrade the JRE, download and run the <em>upgrade_jdk_webapp.sh</em> script. When prompted, provide the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Third-Party Component Compatibility" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[supported version]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247047888 space=MCS version=4 -->
## PAGE 00719: How to approve requirements

- page_id: `247047888`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247047888/How+to+approve+requirements
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-26T21:35:08.252+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > User Guide > Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud > Editing and contextualizing > Model editing use cases
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

One of the ways you can benefit from editing your model in a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document is reviewing and approving Requirements. The following workflow will give you instructions how to do it:

1. . You will need custom Requirements to be able to change their Status (e.g., to Approved).
2. Use the Profile with the custom Requirement subtype in the model you intend to publish for review .
3. In the model, create and define Requirements of the custom Requirement subtype you have created. Converting default requirementsIf you already have default requirements defined in your model, you can convert them to the custom Requirement subtype by [refactoring](https://docs.nomagic.com/MT/?contextKey=refactoring&version=latest&variant=default).
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Publishing documents' space=''] (or the part of the model containing Requirements) to Cameo Collaborator for review and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sharing document links' space=''] with reviewers.
5. .

##### Creating a custom Requirement subtype

If you want to change Requirement status, you need to create a custom Requirement stereotype with the Status tag, as described below.

To create a custom Requirement subtype

1. Create a new SysML v1 project .
2. In the project, Create a Profile element and name it Custom Stereotypes .
3. In the Custom Stereotypes Profile, create a Profile Diagram .
4. In the Profile Diagram, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MED'] and name it Custom Requirement .
5. In the Model Browser, find the Requirement [Class] element (it should be located in the SysML Profile) and drag it to the Profile Diagram.
6. Create a Generalization relationship from the Custom Requirement to the Requirement [Class] .
7. In the same Profile Diagram, create an Enumeration instance, as displayed below, and name it Status . [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_enumeration_instance.png']
8. Create three Enumeration Literals for the Status Enumeration instance, as displayed below, and name them In Progress , Approved , and Disapproved . [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_enumaration_literal.png']
9. Drag the Status Enumeration instance shape to the Custom Requirement Stereotype shape. Your Profile Diagram should look like the one shown below. [ATTACHMENT filename='profile_diagram.png']

Now that you have created a profile with a custom Requirement subtype, you can use it in the model where you want to create custom Requirements.

##### Approving Requirements

To approve Requirements

1. Open the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document with the Requirements you want to review. See the example of a Requirements table below. [ATTACHMENT filename='requirements_table.png']
2. Review the requirements and do one or several of the following actions:
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing element values' space='']Change the **Status** value of the Requirements that do not need any changes to **Approved**. Empty values cannot be editedYou can change only existing Status values. Cameo Collaborator does not allow to enter property values that have not been specified. So make sure that certain Status values (e.g., In Progress) are defined before [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Publishing documents' space=''].
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with comments in Cameo Collaborator' space=''] for items that need to be discussed.
3. Inform the author that you have finished reviewing the document.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>One of the ways you can benefit from editing your model in a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document is reviewing and approving Requirements. The following workflow will give you instructions how to do it:</p><ol><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Creating a custom Requirement subtype"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[In a modeling tool, create a Profile with a custom Requirement subtype]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. You will need custom Requirements to be able to change their Status (e.g., to Approved).</li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/CM/?contextKey=using-server-projects&amp;version=latest">Use the Profile with the custom Requirement subtype in the model you intend to publish for review</a>.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the model, create and define Requirements of the custom requirements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ol><h3>Creating a custom Requirement subtype</h3><p>If you want to change Requirement status, you need to create a custom Requirement stereotype with the Status tag, as described below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a custom Requirement subtype</p><hr /><ol><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/MT/?contextKey=creating-sysml-v1-projects&amp;version=latest&amp;variant=default">Create a new SysML v1 project</a>.</li><li>In the  project, Create a Profile element and name it <em>Custom Stereotypes</em>.</li><li>In the <em>Custom Stereotypes</em> Profile, <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/MT/?contextKey=creating-diagrams&amp;version=latest&amp;variant=default">create a Profile Diagram</a>.</li><li>In the Profile Diagram, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Stereotype" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create a Stereotype]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and name it <em>Custom Requirement</em>.</li><li>In the Model Browser, find the Requirement [Class] element (it should be located in the SysML Profile) and drag it to the Profile Diagram.</li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/MT/?contextKey=creating-a-relationship&amp;version=latest&amp;variant=default">Create a Generalization relationship</a> from the <em>Custom Requirement</em> to the <em>Requirement [Class]</em>.</li><li>In the same Profile Diagram, create an Enumeration instance, as displayed below, and name it <em>Status</em>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="creating_enumeration_instance.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Create three Enumeration Literals for the <em>Status</em> Enumeration instance, as displayed below, and name them <em>In Progress</em>, <em>Approved</em>, and <em>Disapproved</em>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="creating_enumaration_literal.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Drag the <em>Status</em> Enumeration instance shape to the <em>Custom Requirement</em> Stereotype shape. Your Profile Diagram should look like the one shown below.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="profile_diagram.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p>Now that you have created a profile with a custom Requirement subtype, you can use it in the model where you want to create custom Requirements.<br /><br /></p><h3>Approving Requirements</h3><p><br /></p><p>To approve Requirements</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document with the Requirements you want to review. See the example of a Requirements table below.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="requirements_table.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Review the requirements and do one or several of the following actions:</span><br /><ul><li><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Editing element values" /><ac:link-body>Change the <strong>Status</strong> value of the Requirements</ac:link-body></ac:link> that do not need any changes to <strong>Approved</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a68b17de-b140-4c57-8055-083a564eb5f0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Empty values cannot be edited</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can change only existing Status values. Cameo Collaborator does not allow to enter property values that have not been specified. So make sure that certain Status values (e.g., In Progress) are defined before <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Publishing documents" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[publishing a model for editing]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with comments in Cameo Collaborator" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create textual or graphical comments]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for items that need to be discussed.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /></span></li></ul></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Inform the author that you have finished reviewing the document.</span></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247047896 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00720: How to specify Interfaces

- page_id: `247047896`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247047896/How+to+specify+Interfaces
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-11T16:48:47.943+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > User Guide > Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud > Editing and contextualizing > Model editing use cases
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If you are responsible for for a set of interfaces or an interface library, you can enter interface values (e.g. allowed voltages) via a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document.

To specify interface values

1. Open the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document with the Interfaces you want to specify. See the sample table displaying the allowed voltages of Interfaces below. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one or several of the following actions:
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing element values' space='MCS'] (e.g., minimum and maximum voltages). Empty values cannot be editedYou can change only the existing values of Interfaces. Cameo Collaborator does not allow to enter property values that have not been specified. So make sure that certain Interface values are defined before [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Publishing documents' space='MCS'].
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with comments in Cameo Collaborator' space='MCS'] for values that need to be discussed.
3. Inform the author that you have finished updating the model.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>If you are responsible for for a set of interfaces or an interface library, you can enter interface values (e.g. allowed voltages) via a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document.</p><p><br /></p><p>To specify interface values</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Open the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document with the Interfaces you want to specify. See the sample table displaying the allowed voltages of Interfaces below.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="specifying_interfaces.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="How to specify Interfaces" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Do one or several of the following actions:</span><br /><ul><li><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Editing element values" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specify the values of Interfaces]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (e.g., minimum and maximum voltages).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a68b17de-b140-4c57-8055-083a564eb5f0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Empty values cannot be edited</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can change only the existing values of Interfaces. Cameo Collaborator does not allow to enter property values that have not been specified. So make sure that certain Interface values are defined before <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Publishing documents" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[publishing a model for editing]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Working with comments in Cameo Collaborator" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create textual or graphical comments]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for values that need to be discussed.</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></li></ul></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Inform the author that you have finished updating the model.</span></li></ol><p><br /></p>
````
